title: Dittonomics
text:
Dittonomics is a Codex-native research operating system for empirical work.
The Ditto idea is intentional: this system keeps copying useful traits from upstream workflows, then reorganizes them into one portable setup you can keep extending over time. The website is the public manual. The repo is the starter kit. Your local install is the live working system.

title: Dittonomics
text:
Fork / Install / Customize
Start here if you want to use Dittonomics yourself or roll it out for coauthors:

Open the public manual: sebastianritterg.github.io/dittonomics
Fork the GitHub repo: github.com/sebastianritterg/dittonomics
Install the starter locally: copy the repo’s .codex/ into your own ~/.codex/
Add your own writing voice: copy the voice/ templates into ~/voice/ and adapt them privately
Customize per project: keep paper-specific rules in each repo’s AGENTS.md, AGENTS.override.md, and .agents/skills/

title: Dittonomics
text:
What Dittonomics Gives You

an explicit ideation phase before discovery
a research pipeline with explicit phases
worker-critic separation across the stack
Codex-native skills instead of Claude slash-command assumptions
a forkable public .codex/ starter bundle
optional voice files for writing-sensitive workflows
a strategic post-submission revision workflow with roadmap planning and DAG validation
explicit maintenance tools such as checkpoint, resume-context, and verify-edit
a website manual and cheat sheet you can keep open while you work

title: Dittonomics
text:
Start In 20 Minutes

1. Fork or clone the repo
The public repo contains three things you will reuse repeatedly:

the website manual in guide/ and docs/
the public starter bundle in .codex/
the public voice templates in voice/



2. Install the starter bundle locally
Copy or adapt the repo’s .codex/ into your own ~/.codex/.
That gives you:

AGENTS.md
skills/
agents/
workflow references
utility scripts



3. Add an optional voice layer
If you want writing to preserve your own prose register, copy the files in voice/ into ~/voice/ and fill them in privately.
If you do not need a voice layer, skip it.


4. Open a research repo with Codex
In a working project repo, add:

AGENTS.md
optional AGENTS.override.md
optional repo-local skills in .agents/skills/
explorations/ when you need a prototype sandbox



5. Start with ideation when the question is still fuzzy
Before discovery, use:

use $clo-ideate session

That step helps you:

turn a topic or dataset hunch into candidate research questions
check the closest-paper threat early
decide whether to pursue, refine, park, or kill the idea



6. Use the daily loop
Morning:

use $clo-research-tools resume-context

During work:

call the workflow skill that matches the task

End of day:

use $clo-research-tools checkpoint

title: Dittonomics
text:
First Prompts To Try
Use $clo-ideate session on this policy idea and turn it into ranked research questions before discovery.
Use $clo-discover lit to map the literature on teacher value-added and school assignment.
Use $clo-strategize on this research question and propose the main design plus the referee-proof robustness plan.
Use $clo-write intro with current-draft mode and keep econ-intro-writing in charge of structure.
Use $clo-review --peer on the current draft and tell me the highest-risk issues first.
Use $clo-revise roadmap on these referee reports and the submitted paper. Build the execution blocks before we start editing.
Use $clo-research-tools checkpoint for this repo. Capture today's goal, what we finished, blockers, important files, and the best next step.

title: Dittonomics
text:
How The Site Is Organized

Quick Start: what Dittonomics is and how to get running quickly
User Guide: daily usage, ideation-to-submission examples, checkpoint or resume workflow, and how to call skills
Agents: who does what and when the system dispatches each role
Architecture: how the local install, repo overlays, website, and state files fit together
Customization: how to install your own stack, set up voice, and onboard colleagues
Reference: the cheat sheet for skills, utilities, agents, folders, and recurring prompts

title: Dittonomics
text:
Public Home
The public website is:

https://sebastianritterg.github.io/dittonomics/

Keep this site open while you work. It is meant to be the everyday operating manual and cheat sheet for the system.

title: Dittonomics
text:
How To Fork Dittonomics
If you want your own version of the system:

fork the repo on GitHub
clone your fork locally
copy or adapt the repo’s .codex/ starter bundle into your own ~/.codex/
optionally copy the voice/ templates into your private ~/voice/
keep repo-specific rules in each project’s AGENTS.md, AGENTS.override.md, and .agents/skills/
use this website as the shared manual for your own adaptation

The practical split is:

fork the repo when you want your own public or shared Dittonomics variant
copy the starter bundle when you want the user-level install on your machine
use repo overlays when a specific paper or project needs local rules

title: Dittonomics
text:
Public Repo vs Local Install



Layer
Role




guide/
source for the website manual


docs/
rendered site for GitHub Pages


.codex/ in this repo
public starter bundle


~/.codex/
your live local user-level install


~/voice/
your private voice layer


project repo AGENTS.md
repo constitution and working rules


project repo .codex-state/
local checkpoint and resume files

title: Dittonomics
text:
The Ditto Rule
Keep the system open to future additions.
That means:

do not freeze the workflow as if this is the final shape
document new traits as they get added
keep the public starter generic
keep private voice and machine-specific behavior separate
let the website become the shared memory for the team

title: Agents
text:
Dittonomics uses agents to keep specialist work narrow.
The important rule is:

creators create
critics critique
the main Codex session orchestrates

In normal use, you call a skill such as $clo-discover or $clo-write. The skill then decides which agent or worker-critic pair should run.

title: Agents
text:
Ideation









Agent
Role
Typical output
When it runs




ideator
turns rough ideas into candidate research questions
idea brief, ranked question shortlist
$clo-ideate


ideator_critic
pressure-tests early-stage ideas
pursue/refine/park/kill screen
after ideator

title: Agents
text:
Discovery









Agent
Role
Typical output
When it runs




librarian
finds and organizes literature
literature map, annotated bibliography
$clo-discover lit


librarian_critic
checks literature quality
coverage and recency critique
after librarian


explorer
finds and assesses data
data source list, feasibility memo
$clo-discover data


explorer_critic
checks data fit
measurement and validity critique
after explorer

title: Agents
text:
Strategy









Agent
Role
Typical output
When it runs




strategist
proposes identification designs
strategy memo
$clo-strategize


strategist_critic
pressure-tests the design
strategy review report
after strategist

title: Agents
text:
Revision









Agent
Role
Typical output
When it runs




revision_planner
turns real review reports into a strategic roadmap
task graph, roadmap, decision log
$clo-revise roadmap


revision_planner_critic
checks roadmap completeness and sequencing
revision-planning critique
after revision_planner

title: Agents
text:
Execution









Agent
Role
Typical output
When it runs




data_engineer
cleans, wrangles, prepares figures
cleaned data products, figure inputs
$clo-analyze


coder
implements analysis scripts
estimation pipeline, outputs
$clo-analyze


coder_critic
checks execution quality
code review, strategy alignment
after coder or data_engineer

title: Agents
text:
Writing









Agent
Role
Typical output
When it runs




writer
drafts or revises sections
outline, paragraph plan, draft
$clo-write


writer_critic
critiques writing quality
writing review findings
proofread or writing review


econ-intro-writing
governs front-end structure
intro architecture guidance
intro, context, literature, opening stack

title: Agents
text:
Review And Submission









Agent
Role
Typical output
When it runs




editor
calibrates journal posture
editorial synthesis
peer review mode


domain_referee
checks contribution and literature positioning
domain report
peer review mode


methods_referee
checks design and inference
methods report
peer review mode


verifier
checks mechanics and package readiness
verification pass or fail
submission or compile checks

title: Agents
text:
Talks









Agent
Role
Typical output
When it runs




storyteller
builds presentations
talk deck
$clo-talk


storyteller_critic
checks talk quality
talk review report
after storyteller

title: Agents
section: Instead of calling agents directly, call skills
text:
Instead of calling agents directly, call skills



If you want
Use




early idea screening or question generation
$clo-ideate


literature work
$clo-discover lit


data search
$clo-discover data


research design
$clo-strategize


post-submission revision planning
$clo-revise roadmap


coding or output pipeline
$clo-analyze


writing
$clo-write


review
$clo-review


utilities and state management
$clo-research-tools

title: Reference
text:
Use $clo-research-tools resume-context for this repo.



Use $clo-research-tools checkpoint for this repo.



Use $clo-research-tools verify-edit 03_code/src/estimate.py

title: Reference
text: Use $clo-research-tools resume-context for this repo.

title: Reference
text: Use $clo-research-tools checkpoint for this repo.

title: Reference
text: Use $clo-research-tools verify-edit 03_code/src/estimate.py

title: Reference
text:
Ideation
Use $clo-ideate session on [topic or dataset]. Turn it into ranked research questions, check the closest-paper threat, and tell me whether to pursue, refine, park, or kill it.

title: Reference
text:
Discovery
Use $clo-discover lit on [topic]. Start repo-bibliography-first and then broaden if the frontier is moving.

title: Reference
text:
Strategy
Use $clo-strategize on [question]. Give me the main design, the threats, and the robustness plan.

title: Reference
text:
Writing
Use $clo-write [section] with [current-draft/joint-paper/journal-tightening] mode.

title: Reference
text:
Review
Use $clo-review on the latest draft and give findings first.

title: Reference
text:
Revision Roadmap
Use $clo-revise roadmap on [review files] and [submitted paper]. Extract atomic tasks, build the dependency graph, flag disagreement items, and give me execution blocks.

title: Reference
text:
Revision Validation
Use $clo-revise validate [revision_task_graph.json]

title: Reference
text:
Revision Execution
Use $clo-revise execute [revision roadmap] and route the approved tasks back into writing, analysis, and review.

title: Reference
text:
End-of-day
Use $clo-research-tools checkpoint for this repo. Capture today's goal, completed work, blockers, important files, and next step.

title: Reference
text:
Next morning
Use $clo-research-tools resume-context for this repo and tell me where we left off and what to do next.

title: User Guide
text:
Most of the time, you do not call agents directly.
You usually call a workflow skill, and the main Codex session decides whether to:

do the work itself
dispatch a specialist agent
dispatch a worker and then a critic
ask you for a decision at a real fork

That is the main operating idea:

you call skills
Codex orchestrates
agents stay narrow

title: User Guide
text:
Morning: resume cleanly
Start with:
Use $clo-research-tools resume-context for this repo and reconstruct where we left off, the important files, unresolved issues, and the best next action.
What this is meant to do:

read the latest checkpoint in .codex-state/
compare it to current git state
remind you what mattered yesterday
suggest the next action instead of making you rebuild context manually

title: User Guide
section: During the day: use the workflow skills
text:
During the day: use the workflow skills
Use the skill that matches the real task.

Ideation
Use this before discovery when the project is still just:

a topic
a cool dataset
a mechanism hunch
a policy area without a sharp question yet

Use $clo-ideate session on this topic and turn it into ranked research questions with a pursue/refine/park/kill verdict.
Use $clo-ideate rq from this dataset idea and give me the best candidate empirical questions.


Discovery
Use $clo-discover lit on this topic and build the literature map first.
Use $clo-discover data and assess which datasets can support this question.


Strategy
Use $clo-strategize on this question and propose the main design, the key assumptions, and the robustness plan.


Analysis
Use $clo-analyze on this project and focus on building the clean estimation pipeline and the output handoff for writing.


Writing
Use $clo-write intro with current-draft mode and keep econ-intro-writing in charge of structure.
Use $clo-write results and narrate the main tables without overstating causality.


Review
Use $clo-review on this draft and give me the highest-risk findings first.


Revision
Use this when real editor or referee reports already exist.
Use $clo-revise roadmap on these reports and the submitted paper. Extract atomic tasks, validate the dependency logic, and give me execution blocks with go/no-go points.
Use $clo-revise validate quality_reports/revision_tasks_rr1.json
Use $clo-revise execute quality_reports/revision_roadmap_rr1.md and route the approved tasks to the right agents.
Use $clo-revise respond on these reports and the revised draft, using the response matrix and decision log.


Talks and submission
Use $clo-talk seminar to build the deck from the current paper.
Use $clo-submit and check whether this package is actually submission-ready.

title: User Guide
text:
End of day: checkpoint intentionally
Use:
Use $clo-research-tools checkpoint for this repo. Capture today's goal, what we finished, blockers, important files, and the best next step tomorrow.
This is the recommended end-of-day ritual.

title: User Guide
text:
Explicit style
Best when:

you want predictable routing
you are teaching a colleague
you want the website or starter kit to stay easy to follow

Example:
Use $clo-review --peer on the latest draft.

title: User Guide
text:
Natural-language style
Best when:

the task is obvious
you want a conversational workflow
you do not care about naming the exact skill

Example:
Please review the current paper draft like a journal referee and focus on identification and contribution.

title: User Guide
text:
Recommendation
For daily work with colleagues, prefer explicit $clo-* names.
It makes:

the system easier to learn
the website cheat sheet easier to use
routing behavior easier to remember

title: User Guide
text:
What usually activates agents







What you ask for
Typical system response




idea screening or research-question generation
ideator then ideator_critic


literature review
librarian then librarian_critic


data search
explorer then explorer_critic


identification design
strategist then strategist_critic


strategic revision roadmap
revision_planner then revision_planner_critic


coding or data pipeline work
coder or data_engineer, then coder_critic


paper drafting
writer


front-end writing
econ-intro-writing governs structure, then writing follows that contract


proofreading or writing review
writer_critic


peer review
editor, domain_referee, methods_referee

title: User Guide
section: When to mention an agent directly
text:
When to mention an agent directly
Do it when you want to steer the posture.
Examples:
Use $clo-discover lit, but keep it repo-bibliography-first.
Use $clo-review --peer and make the methods_referee especially tough on the event-study design.
Usually you still call the skill, not the raw agent.

title: User Guide
text:
Checkpoint
Purpose:

save the state of a workday
leave structured notes for future you or a colleague

Default location:
.codex-state/
Default files:
.codex-state/
  checkpoints/
  latest.json
  latest.md
What gets stored:

goal
summary
blockers
open questions
important files
next step
git branch and recent commits
directory snapshot

title: User Guide
text:
Resume-context
Purpose:

pick back up quickly the next day
avoid spending the first 10 to 20 minutes reconstructing context

Typical use:
Use $clo-research-tools resume-context for this repo and tell me what I most likely need to do next.

title: User Guide
text:
Verify-edit
Purpose:

run a manual post-edit safety pass
use this instead of hidden background lint hooks

Typical use:
Use $clo-research-tools verify-edit 03_code/src/estimate.py
Use it after:

editing Python, R, or Julia scripts
changing a manuscript build file
touching a critical config file

title: User Guide
text:
Calling the wrong layer
Do not call writer directly unless you have a very specific reason. Usually call $clo-write.

title: User Guide
text:
Treating voice as structure
Voice files shape prose. They do not replace identification logic, evidence discipline, or intro architecture.

title: User Guide
text:
Forgetting the checkpoint
If you stop work without one, the next session becomes unnecessarily expensive.

title: User Guide
text:
Treating references as sole truth
Repo bibliography comes first. Current web or working-paper checks can supplement fast-moving empirical frontiers. Neither should be treated as the only truth source.

title: User Guide
section: Skipping ideation when the question is still vague
text:
Skipping ideation when the question is still vague
Discovery is for formalizing and searching. Ideation is for deciding whether the question deserves that investment.
If the project still sounds more like a direction than a question, start with $clo-ideate.

title: User Guide
section: Jumping into edits before building the revision roadmap
text:
Jumping into edits before building the revision roadmap
When real referee reports arrive, start with $clo-revise roadmap before rewriting sections or running ad hoc analyses.
That step is what:

extracts atomic reviewer tasks
shows which requests actually block others
flags disagreement and go/no-go decisions early

title: Architecture
text:
Dittonomics has four layers that matter in practice.








Layer
Where it lives
What it does




Website manual
guide/ and docs/
teaches humans how the system works


Public starter bundle
.codex/ in this repo
gives a generic forkable install


Live local install
~/.codex/, ~/.agents/skills, ~/voice/
runs your actual working system


Project repo overlay
AGENTS.md, AGENTS.override.md, .agents/skills/
makes behavior repo-specific



The public website explains the system. The starter bundle ships the generic version. Your local install is the live machine. The repo overlay makes each project real.

title: Architecture
text:
Default structure
.codex-state/
  checkpoints/
    2026-04-22T18-30-00Z_end-of-day.json
    2026-04-22T18-30-00Z_end-of-day.md
  latest.json
  latest.md
  resume-context.md

title: Architecture
section: Why both JSON and Markdown exist
text:
Why both JSON and Markdown exist
The JSON files are:

stable
machine-readable
good for scripted recovery later

The Markdown files are:

easy to read
easy to share with a colleague
good for quick manual inspection

title: Architecture
text:
Recommended policy
For working research repos:

keep .codex-state/ local
add it to .gitignore unless you explicitly want to share checkpoints
treat it as operational memory

title: Customization
text:
There are four customization layers:








Layer
Customize this when
Where




public starter
you want to change what colleagues copy by default
repo .codex/


local install
you want your own always-on user setup
~/.codex/, ~/.agents/skills


project repo
one project needs special rules
AGENTS.md, AGENTS.override.md, .agents/skills/


voice
you want prose continuity across sessions
~/voice/

title: Customization
text:
Fork-first option
If you want your own maintained version of Dittonomics for yourself or your team, start by forking the public repo:

https://sebastianritterg.github.io/dittonomics/

Then:

fork the GitHub repository
clone your fork
adapt the public .codex/ starter bundle
publish your own docs site if you want a team-specific manual

Use this route when you want to:

keep extending the system over time
document your team’s conventions
maintain your own website or starter defaults

If you only want the local tooling and do not need your own public variant, you can skip the fork and just copy the starter bundle into ~/.codex/.

title: Customization
text:
1. Start from the public starter
Copy or adapt:
.codex/
into:
~/.codex/
Minimum pieces:

AGENTS.md
skills/
agents/
workflow references
utility scripts

title: Customization
text:
2. Keep machine-specific config local
Your own local machine settings belong in:
~/.codex/config.toml
Examples:

MCP servers
plugin preferences
trust settings
sandbox preferences

title: Customization
section: 3. Keep project-specific rules in the repo
text:
3. Keep project-specific rules in the repo
Use:

AGENTS.md
AGENTS.override.md
.agents/skills/

Do not overload the user-level install with rules that only belong to one paper.

title: Customization
text:
Copy the templates privately
Copy these into ~/voice/:

core_voice.template.md
voice_examples.template.md
econ_paper_register.template.md
style_hierarchy.template.md

Optional:

collaboration_register.template.md
journal_tightening_register.template.md

Then rename them without .template.

title: Customization
text:
What each file does







File
Purpose




core_voice.md
your non-negotiable prose traits


voice_examples.md
examples of sentences, paragraph moves, cadence


econ_paper_register.md
field-appropriate restraint and evidence norms


style_hierarchy.md
precedence rules when files conflict


collaboration_register.md
optional overlay for joint-paper mode


journal_tightening_register.md
optional overlay for tighter journal-facing prose

title: Customization
text:
The voice rule
Your voice layer should shape:

diction
cadence
paragraph texture
compression level

It should not override:

evidence discipline
causal restraint
intro architecture
repo-local terminology rules

title: Customization
text:
A good minimal voice setup
If you want the smallest useful version, start with:

core_voice.md
voice_examples.md
style_hierarchy.md

You can add the others later.

title: Customization
text:
Public or shared

website manual
public starter bundle
project AGENTS.md
generic workflow rules

title: Customization
text:
Private

personal voice files
machine-specific config
private examples you do not want to publish

title: Customization
text:
Project-specific but shared

repo-local overlays
project terminology rules
team conventions for directories and outputs
